claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 11 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 11 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Someone who lives with the child is already claiming

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to start by asking about the child (or children) you're applying for.

**First, let me ask about the child's age:**

How old is the child you're applying for Child Benefit for? 

- Are they **under 16 years old**?
- Are they **between 16 and 19 years old**?
- Or are they **20 or over**?

(If you have multiple children, we can go through each one - let's start with one child first)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and wondering if I'm eligible. I have two children actually - Alex who is 11, and Blake who is 15.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex (11) and Blake (15).

Since both children are under 16 years old, let me check the next requirement. Let me navigate through the assessment for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to check another requirement:

**Are Alex (11) and Blake (15) working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

This is a standard check to ensure they're not working full-time. For children under 16, this is typically not an issue, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all - they're still in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since neither Alex nor Blake are working, let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now the next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

This means benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that would be claimed in the child's own name (not yours as the parent). For most school-age children, this wouldn't apply.

Can you confirm that neither Alex nor Blake receive any benefits in their own name?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, neither of them receive any benefits in their own names.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about apprenticeships:

**Are either Alex (11) or Blake (15) in any type of apprenticeship?**

This includes:
- Any apprenticeships in England (all types would disqualify)
- Modern Apprenticeships in Scotland
- Apprenticeships in Wales (except Foundation Apprenticeships which are approved)

For children aged 11 and 15 who are in school, they're typically not in apprenticeships, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are in any apprenticeships - they're just in regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since neither are in apprenticeships, let me move to the next stage of the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to check about **your residency status** (as the claimant).

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if ANY of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common situation)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do you normally live in the UK?** (This would be the typical situation for most claimants)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK - I'm currently living abroad.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Since you're living abroad, let me check if you meet any of the special circumstances that would still make you eligible.

**Are any of the following true for you?**

1. **Are you a Crown servant** (e.g., working for the UK government, diplomatic service, or armed forces) **living abroad**?

2. **Do you live in certain qualifying countries** where UK Child Benefit can still be claimed (such as specific countries with agreements with the UK)?

Or do you have any of these statuses:
- Settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme?
- Pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme (with work, sufficient resources, or as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national)?

Please let me know which, if any, of these situations applies to you.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of those apply to me. I'm just living abroad as a regular resident there.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying that. Based on the information you've provided, let me navigate to the outcome for the residency requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Assessment Result: INELIGIBLE**

I'm sorry, but based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit** for either Alex or Blake.

### **Summary:**

**For Alex (11 years old):**
- ❌ **Not Eligible** - While Alex meets all the child-related requirements (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship), you cannot claim Child Benefit because you do not meet the UK residency requirements.

**For Blake (15 years old):**
- ❌ **Not Eligible** - While Blake meets all the child-related requirements (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship), you cannot claim Child Benefit because you do not meet the UK residency requirements.

### **Why you're ineligible:**

**UK Residency Requirement Not Met:** To claim Child Benefit, you must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances such as:
- Being a Crown servant living abroad
- Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme (with qualifying criteria)
- Living in certain qualifying countries with agreements with the UK

Since you're living abroad as a regular resident and don't meet any of these special circumstances, you cannot claim Child Benefit at this time.

### **Important Information:**

If your circumstances change and you return to normally live in the UK, you would be able to claim Child Benefit for your children (provided they still meet the age and other requirements at that time).

For more information about Child Benefit eligibility, you can visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/eligibility

Is there anything else you'd like to know or any questions about this decision?

reasonings: Alex (age 11) meets the child-related requirements: under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they live abroad and do not qualify under any special circumstances (Crown servant, EU Settlement Scheme status, or living in qualifying countries). Without meeting the residency requirements, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Alex.

Blake (age 15) meets the child-related requirements: under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they live abroad and do not qualify under any special circumstances (Crown servant, EU Settlement Scheme status, or living in qualifying countries). Without meeting the residency requirements, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two qualifying children (Alex, 11, and Blake, 15) who both meet all the child-related eligibility criteria. However, the claimant is ineligible for Child Benefit because they do not meet the UK residency requirements. They live abroad as a regular resident and do not qualify under any of the special circumstances that would allow claiming while living outside the UK (such as being a Crown servant, having settled/pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or living in certain qualifying countries).

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.
